subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — President vs. Congress in Domestic Policy Making
stimulus: Over time, there has been conflict between the president and Congress over their roles in domestic policy making. Develop an argument as to whether the president or Congress should have more power over domestic policy making.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: Federalist No. 51, Federalist No. 70, Article I of the United States Constitution.)
rubric_criteria: 1 pt (Row A — Thesis/Claim): Responds to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis establishing a line of reasoning about whether the president or Congress should have more power over domestic policy making.

Examples that earn the point: 'The president should have more power because presidents are more effective leaders and are better equipped for the role.' OR 'Presidents should have more power over domestic policy making because they represent all Americans.' OR 'Congress should have more power because its decisions reflect the will of the majority.' OR 'Congress should have more power over domestic policy making since members are the directly elected representatives of the people.'
parts.scoring_notes: Must take a clear position on president vs. Congress in domestic policy. Generic statements about the conflict existing do not earn the point.

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
rubric_criteria: 0 pts: No accurate evidence, or evidence not relevant to the topic.
1 pt (Row B — 1 point): One piece of evidence relevant to the topic.

2 pts (Row B — 2 points): One piece of specific and relevant evidence supports the claim OR two pieces of evidence relevant to the topic (requires Row A for one piece; for two pieces, Row A not required).
3 pts (Row B — 3 points): Two pieces of specific and relevant evidence, one from a listed document (Federalist 51, Federalist 70, or Article I) (requires Row A).

Examples of acceptable evidence: 'Federalist No. 51 discusses the need for a government that checks the ambition of individuals, suggesting Congress should limit executive power.' 'Federalist No. 70 discusses the role of the executive, arguing that unity in the executive brings energy to the government.' 'Article I lists the enumerated powers of Congress, including the power to pass laws and approve the budget.' 'The president has the power to appoint cabinet officials who oversee the bureaucracy.' 'Through iron triangles, Congress can collaborate with the bureaucracy and interest groups to shape domestic policy.' 'Presidents and Vice-Presidents are the only elected officials that represent all Americans.'
parts.scoring_notes: To earn 3 points, must have earned Row A, and one piece of evidence must come from Federalist 51, Federalist 70, or Article I.

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt (Row C — Reasoning): Uses reasoning (classification, process, causation, or comparison) to explain how evidence supports an argument relevant to the prompt.

Examples that earn the point: 'Informal powers give the president the ability to make policies quickly, so the president should have more power over domestic policy making.' 'Senate rules that require a supermajority ensure that the policymaking process produces some level of consensus, so Congress should have more power over domestic policy making.' 'Congress is the closest to the citizens, and a strong executive will interfere with the ability of Congress to establish policies that meet the needs of citizens.'
parts.scoring_notes: Must have provided at least one piece of specific and relevant evidence. Must explain the relationship between evidence and the argument.

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt (Row D — Alternate Perspective): Responds to an opposing perspective using rebuttal or refutation (Note: 2024 rubric no longer includes 'concession'). Must describe an alternate perspective AND rebut or refute it.

Examples that earn the point: 'Representation is important, and while members of Congress represent districts and states, only the president represents all Americans.' 'Some people might argue that Congress is better for domestic policy making because it better represents the people, but better representation could result in Congressional gridlock and slow down the policy making process.' 'The president might make policy quicker, but Congress's slower process is actually better because it allows for more voices to be heard.'
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A. Must describe an alternate perspective AND rebut or refute it — not merely acknowledge it.
